title: Cambridge in Comparison
text:
Overview
This website looks at the Cambridge Central School District (Cambridge CSD, Washington County, New York) alongside nearby districts, using public data from the New York State Education Department (NYSED) and selected other sources. It is one of several analyses in the ccs project; the aim is to put Cambridge’s numbers in context rather than to view them in isolation.

title: Cambridge in Comparison
text:
Comparison groups
Different questions call for different peer sets, so this website uses two groups that can be adjusted as the work develops:

Graph group (5 districts) — used for charts, where a short list keeps lines legible: Cambridge, Greenwich, Salem (all Washington County), Schuylerville (Saratoga County), and Hoosick Falls (Rensselaer County). These are nearby small districts.
Table group (12 districts) — used for tables, which can hold more rows: every Washington County K-12 district plus the two graph-group districts that sit just outside the county (Schuylerville and Hoosick Falls).

The table group is the broader set; the graph group is a readable subset of it. Putnam CSD is left out of the “Washington County K-12” set because it is a K-8 district – it has no high school of its own and pays to send its high-school students elsewhere, so it is not comparable on K-12 measures.







Group
Districts




Graph (charts)
Cambridge, Greenwich, Hoosick Falls, Salem, Schuylerville


Table (tables)
The graph group + Argyle, Fort Ann, Fort Edward, Granville, Hartford, Hudson Falls, Whitehall

title: Cambridge in Comparison
text:
Data and a few cautions
The underlying numbers come from NYSED’s public downloads and other reputable sources; each chapter and the Sources appendix give the specifics. A few things to keep in mind throughout:

Year labels are school-year-ending years. “2025” means the 2024–25 school year. Enrollment is the BEDS-day count (first Wednesday of October).
Teacher counts begin in 2017–18. So time series that depend on teachers (such as students per teacher) start at school-year-ending 2018, even though enrollment runs back to 2015–16.
Students per teacher is an approximation, not official class size. It divides K-12 enrollment by a district-wide teacher headcount (all grades, not full-time-equivalents). It is useful for comparison across districts and over time, but it is not NYSED’s official pupil-teacher or average-class-size figure. See the Sources appendix for detail.
Charter schools are excluded — they have no district-total row in the source data — so these are district-operated figures.

title: 1  Students per teacher
text:
1.1 Average class size
A natural question is how crowded Cambridge’s classrooms are compared with nearby districts. The measure most people picture is class size — the number of students in a typical classroom. NYSED does publish an average class size for each district, and we look at it first; but it is reported only for a handful of specific grades and subjects (there is no single official number) and only since 2018–19. So for a longer, more complete view we also use K-12 students per teacher: a district’s K-12 enrollment divided by its number of teachers.
Students per teacher is a useful staffing comparison, but it is not class size, and it is always lower than class size — because it counts every teacher (including part-time teachers and specialists who don’t lead a regular class), not just the teachers standing in front of a full classroom. Read it as a staffing ratio, not a count of children in a room.
A reminder from the Overview: the teacher figure is a district-wide headcount (all grades, not full-time-equivalents), so this is an approximate measure for comparing districts and tracking change over time. Lower means fewer students per teacher.
Start with the measure people actually picture. NYSED reports an average class size for each district — but only for a specific set of classes (the early elementary grades and the courses tied to State tests), and only since 2018–19. There is no single official “district class size,” so the line below is the median across all of a district’s reported classes each year: a robust middle value that a stray two-student elective can’t throw off.
Cambridge has the smallest typical class size of the group.
Code
g = cc.class_size_median_for(cc.GRAPH_GROUP).drop_nulls("median_class_size")
cambridge = g.filter(pl.col("district") == cc.FOCUS)

(
    ggplot(g, aes("year_end", "median_class_size", color="district"))
    + geom_line(size=1)
    + geom_point(size=1.8)
    + geom_line(cambridge, size=2.4)  # emphasize the focus district
    + scale_color_manual(
        values=cc.PALETTE, name="", limits=cc.district_order(cc.GRAPH_GROUP)
    )
    + scale_x_continuous(breaks=list(range(2019, 2026)))
    + labs(
        x="School year ending",
        y="Median class size",
        # Title states only the facts; the interpretive headline lives above the
        # figure in the prose (see the project's graph-title convention).
        title="Class size",
    )
    + cc.theme_ccs()
)








Figure 1.1: Median of NYSED’s reported average class sizes across all measured classes (early elementary grades and courses aligned to State tests), by district and school year ending, 2019 (2018–19) through 2025 (2024–25). NYSED publishes class size only for these specific classes and only since 2018–19; the median summarizes them and resists small-section outliers. The 2020–21 (2021) figures are affected by pandemic reporting disruption — Salem’s dip that year is a data artifact, not a real change. Source: NYSED Student & Educator (Average Class Size) database.
Across these years Cambridge’s median class size runs about 11 to 15 students — consistently the lowest or near-lowest of the five, and several students below Schuylerville, Salem and Hoosick Falls. (Salem’s sharp dip in 2021 is a pandemic-year reporting artifact, not a real change.) That is the same story the staffing ratio tells next, seen at the level of actual classrooms: smaller classes, fewer students per teacher.
Two cautions before leaning on it too hard. NYSED measures class size for only a handful of classes, and the counts are small — a single combined section or a lightly-enrolled elective can swing a small district’s numbers from year to year. And the series only starts in 2018–19. For a longer, more complete view we turn to students per teacher — which exists for every district back to 2017–18, though, as the next section explains, it is a staffing ratio, not class size.

title: 1  Students per teacher
text:
1.1.1 A sharper cut: grades 3–8 core classes
The median above mixes very different classes — small high-school electives with larger elementary sections — and is swayed by which classes each district happens to report. A cleaner, apples-to-apples comparison restricts to the twelve core academic classes every district teaches and reports: English and Math in grades 3 through 8. (NYSED relabeled these classes in 2021; the build unifies the old and new names.)
On the same twelve classes, Cambridge’s are the smallest in the group — by several students per class.


Code
core = cc.class_size_for(cc.GRAPH_GROUP, tier="grades_3_8", subjects=["ELA", "Math"])

# Long -> wide (years become columns), Washington County districts first.
wide = (
    core.with_columns(
        pl.when(pl.col("region") == "Washington County").then(0).otherwise(1).alias("_rank")
    )
    .pivot(values="class_size", index=["_rank", "region", "district"], on="year_end")
    .sort(["_rank", "district"])
    .drop("_rank")
)
year_cols = [c for c in wide.columns if c.isdigit()]
wide = wide.with_columns((pl.col("2025") - pl.col("2019")).alias("chg"))

(
    GT(wide, rowname_col="district", groupname_col="region")
    .tab_header(
        title="Class size: grades 3–8 core (English & Math)",
        subtitle="Median across the twelve grade-3–8 ELA and Math classes, by school year ending",
    )
    .fmt_number(columns=year_cols + ["chg"], decimals=1)
    .tab_spanner(label="School year ending", columns=year_cols)
    .cols_label(chg=md("Change<br>2019→2025"))
    .sub_missing(missing_text="—")
    .tab_style(
        style=[style.fill(color="#FCEFB4"), style.text(weight="bold")],
        locations=[
            loc.body(rows=pl.col("district") == cc.FOCUS),
            loc.stub(rows=pl.col("district") == cc.FOCUS),
        ],
    )
    .tab_source_note(
        md(
            "**Source:** NYSED Student & Educator database, Average Class Size "
            "(roster-based: students ÷ sections). Each district-year is the median "
            "across its grades 3–8 English and Math classes; NYSED's 2019–20 and "
            "2021+ labels for these classes are unified in the build. Year = "
            "school-year-ending (2025 = 2024–25)."
        )
    )
)




Table 1.1: Median average class size across the twelve grades 3–8 core academic classes (English and Math, grades 3–8) by district and school year ending, 2019 (2018–19) through 2025 (2024–25) — a like-for-like basket every district reports. Salem’s 2021 value reflects pandemic-year reporting disruption. Source: NYSED Student & Educator (Average Class Size) database.

Each district-year is the median across its grades 3–8 English and Math classes; NYSED's 2019–20 and 2021+ labels for these classes are unified in the build. Year = school-year-ending (2025 = 2024–25).












Cambridge’s grades 3–8 core classes run about 10 to 16 students, several below the other four districts in most years (Salem’s dip to 9 in 2021 is the same pandemic-reporting artifact). This is the most like-for-like class-size comparison the NYSED data supports; the broad median above and the students-per-teacher ratio below tell the same story with wider coverage.


1.1.2 High school: the Regents courses
High school is measured not by grade but as a set of specific Regents courses, and the picture changes. Sections are smaller and more variable — a capstone like Physics often runs 6–8 students — so we read it two ways: a clean gateway basket of the three courses nearly every student takes (Algebra I, English / ELA III, and Biology), and the median across all ten reported Regents courses.
At the high-school level the gap largely closes — Cambridge’s classes are middle-of-the-pack, not distinctly smaller.


Code
gw = cc.class_size_for(
    cc.GRAPH_GROUP, tier="high_school", classes=["Algebra I", "ELA III", "Biology"]
).drop_nulls("class_size")
cambridge = gw.filter(pl.col("district") == cc.FOCUS)

(
    ggplot(gw, aes("year_end", "class_size", color="district"))
    + geom_line(size=1)
    + geom_point(size=1.8)
    + geom_line(cambridge, size=2.4)  # emphasize the focus district
    + scale_color_manual(
        values=cc.PALETTE, name="", limits=cc.district_order(cc.GRAPH_GROUP)
    )
    + scale_x_continuous(breaks=list(range(2019, 2026)))
    + labs(
        x="School year ending",
        y="Median class size",
        title="High school class size: gateway courses",
    )
    + cc.theme_ccs()
)








Figure 1.2: Median average class size across the three high-school ‘gateway’ Regents courses nearly every student takes — Algebra I, English (ELA III), and Biology — by district and school year ending, 2019 through 2025. High-school sections are small in districts this size, so single years are volatile. Source: NYSED Student & Educator (Average Class Size) database.





Across all ten Regents courses the picture is similar but noisier:


Code
hs = cc.class_size_for(cc.GRAPH_GROUP, tier="high_school")
wide = (
    hs.with_columns(
        pl.when(pl.col("region") == "Washington County").then(0).otherwise(1).alias("_rank")
    )
    .pivot(values="class_size", index=["_rank", "region", "district"], on="year_end")
    .sort(["_rank", "district"])
    .drop("_rank")
)
year_cols = [c for c in wide.columns if c.isdigit()]

(
    GT(wide, rowname_col="district", groupname_col="region")
    .tab_header(
        title="High school class size: all ten Regents courses",
        subtitle="Median across the reported Regents courses, by school year ending",
    )
    .fmt_number(columns=year_cols, decimals=1)
    .tab_spanner(label="School year ending", columns=year_cols)
    .sub_missing(missing_text="—")
    .tab_style(
        style=[style.fill(color="#FCEFB4"), style.text(weight="bold")],
        locations=[
            loc.body(rows=pl.col("district") == cc.FOCUS),
            loc.stub(rows=pl.col("district") == cc.FOCUS),
        ],
    )
    .tab_source_note(
        md(
            "**Source:** NYSED Student & Educator database, Average Class Size "
            "(roster-based: students ÷ sections). Label variants of a course across "
            "curriculum frameworks are unified in the build. Year = "
            "school-year-ending (2025 = 2024–25)."
        )
    )
)




Table 1.2: Median average class size across all ten reported high-school Regents courses (Algebra I/II, Geometry, ELA III, Biology, Chemistry, Earth Science, Physics, US History & Gov’t, World History) by district and school year ending, 2019–2025. High-school sections are small and some courses go unreported in a given year, so single values are volatile. Source: NYSED Student & Educator (Average Class Size) database.

Two things stand out. First, the small-class advantage Cambridge shows in the elementary and middle grades fades in high school: by the recent years its gateway-course classes (~14–15) sit in the middle of the group — Greenwich runs a touch lower, and only Salem is clearly higher. Second, the high-school figures are volatile in districts this small: Cambridge’s 2019–20 lows and Salem’s swings (8 in 2021, 24 in 2024) reflect a handful of sections, not real changes of that size. Read the high-school numbers as broad-brush.

title: 1  Students per teacher
text:
1.2 Students per teacher, over time
The chart below tracks the graph group from school-year-ending 2018 (the first year teacher counts exist) through 2025.
Cambridge now has the fewest students per teacher of the group.


Code
g = (
    cc.panel_for(cc.GRAPH_GROUP)  # 5 districts, 2018+
    .select("district", "year_end", "k12_students_per_teacher")
    .drop_nulls()
)
cambridge = g.filter(pl.col("district") == cc.FOCUS)

(
    ggplot(g, aes("year_end", "k12_students_per_teacher", color="district"))
    + geom_line(size=1)
    + geom_point(size=1.8)
    + geom_line(cambridge, size=2.4)  # emphasize the focus district
    + scale_color_manual(
        values=cc.PALETTE, name="", limits=cc.district_order(cc.GRAPH_GROUP)
    )
    + scale_x_continuous(breaks=list(range(2018, 2026)))
    + labs(
        x="School year ending",
        y="K–12 students per teacher",
        # Title states only the facts; the interpretive headline lives above the
        # figure in the prose (see the project's graph-title convention).
        title="Students per teacher",
    )
    + cc.theme_ccs()
)








Figure 1.3: K–12 students per teacher by school year ending, 2018 (2017–18) through 2025 (2024–25). Lower means fewer students per teacher. Source: NYSED Enrollment and Student & Educator databases.





In 2017–18 only Salem had a lower ratio than Cambridge. Cambridge’s ratio then drifted down — its K-12 enrollment fell about 15% (from 869 to 741) while its teacher headcount stayed roughly flat — and by the two most recent years Cambridge has the lowest students-per-teacher ratio of the five. A jump in reported teachers in 2023–24 pushed its 2024 figure especially low (8.2). Schuylerville and Greenwich actually fell more over the full period (about 2.0 and 1.9, versus Cambridge’s 1.5), but from much higher starting points, so they remain above Cambridge.

title: 1  Students per teacher
text:
1.3 Another view: teachers per 100 students
Students per teacher and teachers per 100 students are the very same data seen from opposite ends. The first asks how many students share each teacher; the second asks how many teachers a district employs for every 100 students. One is just the other turned upside-down — but flipping it puts staffing, rather than crowding, in the foreground, and now a higher line means more teachers per student.
Seen this way, Cambridge now employs the most teachers per 100 students of the group.


Code
g = (
    cc.panel_for(cc.GRAPH_GROUP)  # 5 districts, 2018+
    .select("district", "year_end", "num_teachers", "k12_enrollment")
    .drop_nulls()
    .with_columns(
        (pl.col("num_teachers") / pl.col("k12_enrollment") * 100).alias(
            "teachers_per_100_students"
        )
    )
)
cambridge = g.filter(pl.col("district") == cc.FOCUS)

(
    ggplot(g, aes("year_end", "teachers_per_100_students", color="district"))
    + geom_line(size=1)
    + geom_point(size=1.8)
    + geom_line(cambridge, size=2.4)  # emphasize the focus district
    + scale_color_manual(
        values=cc.PALETTE, name="", limits=cc.district_order(cc.GRAPH_GROUP)
    )
    + scale_x_continuous(breaks=list(range(2018, 2026)))
    + labs(
        x="School year ending",
        y="K–12 teachers per 100 students",
        # Title states only the facts; the interpretive headline lives above the
        # figure in the prose (see the project's graph-title convention).
        title="Teachers per 100 students",
    )
    + cc.theme_ccs()
)








Figure 1.4: K–12 teachers per 100 students by school year ending, 2018 (2017–18) through 2025 (2024–25). Higher means more teachers per student. Computed as a district’s teacher headcount ÷ its K-12 enrollment × 100. Source: NYSED Enrollment and Student & Educator databases.





This is the mirror image of the first chart: wherever a district’s students-per-teacher ratio fell, its teachers-per-100-students rose. In 2017–18 only Salem employed more teachers per 100 students than Cambridge. Cambridge’s line then climbed — its K-12 enrollment fell about 15% while its teacher headcount held roughly steady — and by the two most recent years Cambridge has the most teachers per 100 students of the five (about 11.5 in 2024–25). The jump in reported teachers in 2023–24 pushed its 2024 figure especially high (12.2). The two larger districts, Schuylerville and Hoosick Falls, stay lowest, around 9 to 9.6 teachers per 100 students.
The table below puts the two ends of Cambridge’s story side by side, and shows the arithmetic behind the rise.
Cambridge’s enrollment fell about 15% while its teacher headcount held steady, so teachers per 100 students rose about 17%.


Code
# cc.staffing_table() builds this for any district (by NYSED code or label); see
# comparisons.py. Pass e.g. "Greenwich" or "64080104" for another district.
cc.staffing_table(cc.FOCUS)




Table 1.3: Cambridge K–12 enrollment, teachers, and teachers per 100 students by school year ending, 2018 (2017–18) through 2025 (2024–25), with the change and percent change from 2018 to 2025.

title: 1  Students per teacher
text:
1.4 The full comparison group
The table widens the lens to all twelve table-group districts. Columns are school-years-ending; the final column is the change from 2018 to 2025 (a negative value means fewer students per teacher than in 2018). Cambridge is highlighted.


Code
t = cc.panel_for(cc.TABLE_GROUP)

# Long -> wide (years become columns), Washington County districts first,
# then alphabetical within each region.
wide = (
    t.select("region", "district", "year_end", "k12_students_per_teacher")
    .with_columns(
        pl.when(pl.col("region") == "Washington County")
        .then(0)
        .otherwise(1)
        .alias("_rank")
    )
    .pivot(
        values="k12_students_per_teacher",
        index=["_rank", "region", "district"],
        on="year_end",
    )
    .sort(["_rank", "district"])
    .drop("_rank")
)

year_cols = [c for c in wide.columns if c.isdigit()]
wide = wide.with_columns(
    (pl.col("2025") - pl.col("2018")).alias("chg")
)

(
    GT(wide, rowname_col="district", groupname_col="region")
    .tab_header(
        title="K–12 students per teacher",
        subtitle="School-year-ending 2018 (2017–18) through 2025 (2024–25)",
    )
    .fmt_number(columns=year_cols + ["chg"], decimals=1)
    .tab_spanner(label="School year ending", columns=year_cols)
    .cols_label(chg=md("Change<br>2018→2025"))
    .sub_missing(missing_text="—")
    .tab_style(
        style=[style.fill(color="#FCEFB4"), style.text(weight="bold")],
        locations=[
            loc.body(rows=pl.col("district") == cc.FOCUS),
            loc.stub(rows=pl.col("district") == cc.FOCUS),
        ],
    )
    .tab_source_note(
        md(
            "**Source:** NYSED Enrollment database (K-12 enrollment) and "
            "Student & Educator database (teacher counts). "
            "Ratio = district K-12 enrollment ÷ teacher headcount; an "
            "approximate comparison measure, not official class size. "
            "Year = school-year-ending (2025 = 2024–25)."
        )
    )
)




Table 1.4: K–12 students per teacher by district and school year ending.

Source: NYSED Enrollment database (K-12 enrollment) and Student & Educator database (teacher counts). Ratio = district K-12 enrollment ÷ teacher headcount; an approximate comparison measure, not official class size. Year = school-year-ending (2025 = 2024–25).












Across the wider group, Cambridge ends among the lowest ratios — close to Salem — while the rest of Washington County’s K-12 districts span a wider range. The table is also a reminder that these districts differ greatly in size (compare small districts like Fort Edward with larger ones like Hudson Falls and Granville), which shapes how much a single hire or a small enrollment swing moves the ratio.

title: 1  Students per teacher
section: 1.5 A national cross-check: NCES teacher FTE
text:
1.5 A national cross-check: NCES teacher FTE
The ratio above has two limitations worth testing. NYSED reports a teacher headcount, whereas the standard pupil-teacher ratio — the one NCES, the OECD and most cross-state comparisons use — divides enrollment by teacher full-time-equivalents (FTE). And it comes from a single state source. The federal Common Core of Data (CCD) offers an independent check: it reports teacher FTE and enrollment for every district. (CCD labels a school year by its fall term, so its years are shifted here to match NYSED’s school-year-ending labels.)
First the diagnostic question — do the two measures agree for Cambridge?
The two measures never differ by more than about two-thirds of a student per teacher, and both fall sharply in 2023–24.


Code
ny = (
    cc.panel_for(cc.GRAPH_GROUP)
    .filter(pl.col("district") == cc.FOCUS)
    .select("year_end", pl.col("k12_students_per_teacher").alias("ratio"))
    .drop_nulls()
    .with_columns(pl.lit("NYSED (teacher headcount)").alias("source"))
)
ccd_cam = (
    cc.ccd_ratio_for(cc.GRAPH_GROUP)
    .filter((pl.col("district") == cc.FOCUS) & (pl.col("year_end") >= 2018))
    .select("year_end", pl.col("ccd_students_per_teacher").alias("ratio"))
    .drop_nulls()
    .with_columns(pl.lit("NCES (teacher FTE)").alias("source"))
)
both = pl.concat(
    [
        ny.with_columns(pl.col("year_end").cast(pl.Int64)),
        ccd_cam.with_columns(pl.col("year_end").cast(pl.Int64)),
    ]
)
source_colors = {
    "NYSED (teacher headcount)": "#C8102E",
    "NCES (teacher FTE)": "#2C6E9B",
}

(
    ggplot(both, aes("year_end", "ratio", color="source"))
    + geom_line(size=1.2)
    + geom_point(size=2)
    + scale_color_manual(values=source_colors, name="")
    + scale_x_continuous(breaks=list(range(2018, 2026)))
    + labs(
        x="School year ending",
        y="Students per teacher",
        title="Cambridge students per teacher: headcount vs. FTE",
    )
    + cc.theme_ccs()
)








Figure 1.5: Cambridge students per teacher: NYSED (teacher headcount) vs. NCES Common Core of Data (teacher FTE), by school year ending. Sources: NYSED Enrollment and Student & Educator databases; NCES CCD via the Urban Institute Education Data Portal.





The two lines stay close because, in small districts, a teacher headcount and its full-time-equivalent are nearly the same — there are few part-timers. More important, the independent FTE measure shows the same sharp decline in 2023–24, so that turning point is not an artifact of how NYSED counts teachers.
Having confirmed the two agree, the next chart and table repeat the earlier views using the NCES FTE-based ratio — the version directly comparable to other states and to national figures.
On the NCES measure too, Cambridge has the lowest ratio of the group in the two most recent years.


Code
gc = (
    cc.ccd_ratio_for(cc.GRAPH_GROUP)
    .filter(pl.col("year_end") >= 2018)
    .select("district", "year_end", pl.col("ccd_students_per_teacher").alias("ratio"))
    .drop_nulls()
)
cambridge_ccd = gc.filter(pl.col("district") == cc.FOCUS)

(
    ggplot(gc, aes("year_end", "ratio", color="district"))
    + geom_line(size=1)
    + geom_point(size=1.8)
    + geom_line(cambridge_ccd, size=2.4)  # emphasize the focus district
    + scale_color_manual(
        values=cc.PALETTE, name="", limits=cc.district_order(cc.GRAPH_GROUP)
    )
    + scale_x_continuous(breaks=list(range(2018, 2026)))
    + labs(
        x="School year ending",
        y="Students per teacher (NCES FTE)",
        title="District enrollment ÷ teacher FTE (NCES CCD), 2017–18 to 2024–25",
    )
    + cc.theme_ccs()
)








Figure 1.6: NCES (CCD) FTE-based students per teacher by school year ending. Source: NCES Common Core of Data via the Urban Institute Education Data Portal.







Code
tc = cc.ccd_ratio_for(cc.TABLE_GROUP).filter(pl.col("year_end").is_between(2018, 2025))

wide_c = (
    tc.with_columns(
        pl.when(pl.col("region") == "Washington County")
        .then(0)
        .otherwise(1)
        .alias("_rank")
    )
    .pivot(
        values="ccd_students_per_teacher",
        index=["_rank", "region", "district"],
        on="year_end",
    )
    .sort(["_rank", "district"])
    .drop("_rank")
)

year_cols_c = [c for c in wide_c.columns if c.isdigit()]
wide_c = wide_c.with_columns((pl.col("2025") - pl.col("2018")).alias("chg"))

(
    GT(wide_c, rowname_col="district", groupname_col="region")
    .tab_header(
        title="Students per teacher (NCES FTE basis)",
        subtitle="School-year-ending 2018 (2017–18) through 2025 (2024–25)",
    )
    .fmt_number(columns=year_cols_c + ["chg"], decimals=1)
    .tab_spanner(label="School year ending", columns=year_cols_c)
    .cols_label(chg=md("Change<br>2018→2025"))
    .sub_missing(missing_text="—")
    .tab_style(
        style=[style.fill(color="#FCEFB4"), style.text(weight="bold")],
        locations=[
            loc.body(rows=pl.col("district") == cc.FOCUS),
            loc.stub(rows=pl.col("district") == cc.FOCUS),
        ],
    )
    .tab_source_note(
        md(
            "**Source:** NCES Common Core of Data (teacher FTE and enrollment), "
            "via the Urban Institute Education Data Portal. Ratio = district "
            "enrollment ÷ teacher full-time-equivalents — the nationally "
            "standard pupil-teacher ratio. CCD years are shifted to "
            "school-year-ending (2025 = 2024–25)."
        )
    )
)




Table 1.5: NCES (CCD) FTE-based students per teacher by district and school year ending.

Source: NCES Common Core of Data (teacher FTE and enrollment), via the Urban Institute Education Data Portal. Ratio = district enrollment ÷ teacher full-time-equivalents — the nationally standard pupil-teacher ratio. CCD years are shifted to school-year-ending (2025 = 2024–25).












One difference to keep in mind: the NCES ratio divides total district enrollment (which includes pre-K) by teacher FTE, while the NYSED ratio earlier uses K-12 enrollment and a headcount. The two therefore differ slightly in level, but they agree on the pattern — which is what the comparison turns on.

title: 2  School district spending
text:
2.1 What “spending per pupil” means here
How much does each district spend to educate a student, and how does Cambridge compare? This chapter opens with the broadest summary — current operating spending per pupil — and will build toward the major categories of spending in later sections.
A district’s spending can be measured several ways, and the choice changes the number a lot. This chapter uses current operating spending: the cost of running schools in a year — salaries, benefits, classroom and BOCES services, food service, and federally funded programs — divided by K-12 enrollment.
It deliberately leaves out two things that make year-to-year comparisons misleading:
This is close to the “current spending per pupil” measure the U.S. Census and the National Center for Education Statistics use to compare districts and states. The dollars come from the New York State Comptroller’s financial-reporting data and are not adjusted for inflation, so part of the upward drift over time reflects rising prices rather than rising real resources.

title: 2  School district spending
text:
Capital projects (new buildings, roofs, buses) and the debt that pays for them. These arrive in large, irregular lumps — one building project can briefly multiply a district’s total spending. Salem’s all-funds spending hit about $87,000 per pupil in 2021–22 for this reason, against current operating spending of about $26,000 that year.
Transfers between a district’s own funds, which would otherwise be counted twice.

title: 2  School district spending
text:
2.2 Spending per pupil, over time
Cambridge spends near the top of the group per pupil — consistently second only to Salem.


Code
g = (
    cc.spending_for(cc.GRAPH_GROUP)
    .filter(pl.col("year_end") >= 2016)
    .select("district", "year_end", "current_pp")
    .drop_nulls()
)
cambridge = g.filter(pl.col("district") == cc.FOCUS)

(
    ggplot(g, aes("year_end", "current_pp", color="district"))
    + geom_line(size=1)
    + geom_point(size=1.8)
    + geom_line(cambridge, size=2.4)  # emphasize the focus district
    + scale_color_manual(
        values=cc.PALETTE, name="", limits=cc.district_order(cc.GRAPH_GROUP)
    )
    + scale_x_continuous(breaks=list(range(2016, 2026)))
    + scale_y_continuous(labels=_dollars)
    + labs(
        x="School year ending",
        y="Current operating spending per pupil",
        title="Current operating spending per K-12 pupil, 2015–16 to 2024–25",
    )
    + cc.theme_ccs()
)








Figure 2.1: Current operating spending per K–12 pupil (nominal dollars) by school year ending, 2016 (2015–16) through 2025 (2024–25). Source: NYS Comptroller local-government financial data; K-12 enrollment from NYSED.





The two highest-spending districts per pupil — Cambridge and Salem — are also the two smallest by enrollment. That is the cost-side mirror of the students-per-teacher picture: a small district spreads fixed costs (a principal, a building, a bus run) over fewer students, so its spending per pupil runs high even when its total budget is modest. Every district’s figure rises over the decade, but remember these are nominal dollars — much of that climb is inflation.

title: 2  School district spending
text:
2.3 The full comparison group
The table widens the lens to all twelve table-group districts. Columns are school-years-ending; the final column is the change from 2016 to 2025 (in nominal dollars). Cambridge is highlighted.


Code
t = cc.spending_for(cc.TABLE_GROUP).filter(pl.col("year_end").is_between(2016, 2025))

wide = (
    t.select("region", "district", "year_end", "current_pp")
    .with_columns(
        pl.when(pl.col("region") == "Washington County")
        .then(0)
        .otherwise(1)
        .alias("_rank")
    )
    .pivot(values="current_pp", index=["_rank", "region", "district"], on="year_end")
    .sort(["_rank", "district"])
    .drop("_rank")
)

year_cols = [c for c in wide.columns if c.isdigit()]
wide = wide.with_columns((pl.col("2025") - pl.col("2016")).alias("chg"))

(
    GT(wide, rowname_col="district", groupname_col="region")
    .tab_header(
        title="Current operating spending per pupil",
        subtitle="School-year-ending 2016 (2015–16) through 2025 (2024–25), nominal dollars",
    )
    .fmt_currency(columns=year_cols + ["chg"], decimals=0)
    .tab_spanner(label="School year ending", columns=year_cols)
    .cols_label(chg=md("Change<br>2016→2025"))
    .sub_missing(missing_text="—")
    .tab_style(
        style=[style.fill(color="#FCEFB4"), style.text(weight="bold")],
        locations=[
            loc.body(rows=pl.col("district") == cc.FOCUS),
            loc.stub(rows=pl.col("district") == cc.FOCUS),
        ],
    )
    .tab_source_note(
        md(
            "**Source:** New York State Comptroller local-government financial "
            "data (expenditures); K-12 enrollment from NYSED. Current operating "
            "spending = General, Special Aid and School Lunch funds, excluding "
            "capital outlay, debt service and interfund transfers. Nominal "
            "dollars; year = school-year-ending (2025 = 2024–25)."
        )
    )
)




Table 2.1: Current operating spending per K–12 pupil by district and school year ending (nominal dollars).

Current operating spending = General, Special Aid and School Lunch funds, excluding capital outlay, debt service and interfund transfers. Nominal dollars; year = school-year-ending (2025 = 2024–25).












Across the wider group the same pattern holds: the smallest districts tend to post the highest spending per pupil, and Cambridge sits near the top. Later sections will open up what the money buys — the major categories of spending — and add the general-fund-only and total-all-funds views for context.

title: Sources & references
text:
Data sources index
All figures on this website come from public data. This appendix documents each source, notes the main caveats, and lists full references (APA style) at the end. Citation keys are given in brackets so they can be reused in the text.
Every dataset the project uses (or is weighing). Raw files and a full provenance record for each live under data/raw/<source>/ in a tracked SOURCE.md; the scheme is described in data/README.md. Years are school-year-ending (2025 = 2024–25).

title: Sources & references
text:
NYSED data files
The enrollment and staffing numbers are built from two New York State Education Department databases, both downloaded from https://data.nysed.gov/downloads.php and distributed as annual Microsoft Access files:

Enrollment database (New York State Education Department, 2025a) — the BEDS Day Enrollment table, which gives K-12 enrollment by district. Coverage on this website: school years 2015–16 through 2024–25. Enrollment is the BEDS-day count (first Wednesday of October).
Student & Educator (“STUDED”) database (New York State Education Department, 2025b) — the Staff table, which gives teacher, principal, counselor, and social-worker counts plus teacher attendance and turnover. Coverage: school years 2017–18 through 2024–25. Because teacher counts start in 2017–18, any measure that uses teachers (such as students per teacher) begins at school-year-ending

The same database’s Average Class Size table — roster-based class sizes (students ÷ sections) for K-2 and the State-tested grade/subject and Regents courses, school years 2018–19 through 2024–25 — backs the class-size charts; it is measured from course rosters, not teacher counts, and NYSED’s pre-2018-19 (teacher-form) class-size data uses a different method and is excluded.


These were stacked into a district-by-year panel by src/build_enrollment_teachers.py; the dataset’s own SOURCE.md records the build in full.
Caveats worth repeating. num_teachers is a district-wide headcount (all grades, not full-time-equivalents), while k12_enrollment is K-12 only. The derived students-per-teacher ratio is therefore an approximate measure for comparison across districts and over time — not NYSED’s official pupil-teacher ratio or average class size. Charter schools are excluded (they have no district-total row), and a small number of districts reorganized within the window (statewide, not in this comparison group).

title: Sources & references
text:
Student test results (NYSED grades 3-8 assessments)
To look at student performance, the project uses NYSED’s annual Report Card Database (also from https://data.nysed.gov/downloads.php, distributed as Microsoft Access files). These databases hold the results of New York’s grades 3-8 English Language Arts (ELA) and Mathematics tests — the statewide exams every public-school student takes each spring — reported for each district (and for statewide, county, and “similar-needs” peer groupings) and broken out by student group. Coverage built so far: school years 2015–16 through 2024–25 (ten years). The same databases also contain Regents and other results, which may be added later. The ten annual files were stitched into one district-by-year panel by src/build_assessments.py; full provenance is in data/raw/nysed_report_card/SOURCE.md.
The headline measure is the percentage of students scoring at or above proficiency (performance Level 3 or higher); the data also include the number tested and the average score. Two cautions matter a great deal for reading trends:

Spring 2020 is missing and 2021 is unusual. The grades 3-8 tests were cancelled in spring 2020 because of COVID-19, so that year has no data; in spring 2021 only about half of students were tested, so 2021’s numbers are not representative.
The tests were redesigned twice, so scores don’t compare across the breaks. New York set new tests and new passing standards in 2018 and again in 2023 (the Next Generation Learning Standards). Results are comparable within each of three periods — 2015–2017, 2018–2022, and 2023–2025 — but a change in the proficiency rate across one of those boundaries reflects the new standard as much as any change in student learning. Comparisons between districts in the same year are unaffected by this and are the most reliable use of the data.

title: Sources & references
section: National pupil–teacher ratios (NCES Common Core of Data)
text:
National pupil–teacher ratios (NCES Common Core of Data)
To cross-check the headcount-based ratio above against a full-time-equivalent measure, the project pulls the federal Common Core of Data (CCD) school- district directory — which reports teacher FTE — through the Urban Institute’s Education Data Portal (https://educationdata.urban.org), which mirrors NCES. CCD labels a year by its fall term, so it is shifted to this project’s school-year-ending convention (CCD 2024 → 2024–25).

title: Sources & references
text:
School district finance (NYS Comptroller)
Per-pupil spending comes from the New York State Comptroller’s (OSC) local-government financial data — annual revenue and expenditure detail, by account code, that every district reports in its Annual Update Document under the state Uniform System of Accounts. Funds are identified by the leading letter of the account code (General, Special Aid, School Lunch, Capital, Debt Service). The current operating spending per pupil measure used in the spending chapter keeps the General, Special Aid, and School Lunch funds and excludes capital outlay, debt service, and transfers between funds, to approximate the U.S. Census / NCES basis for comparing districts. The fiscal year ends June 30 and matches the school-year-ending label (no off-by-one). Dollars are nominal (not adjusted for inflation). Full provenance: data/raw/osc_school_finance/SOURCE.md.

title: Sources & references
text:
Property Tax Report Card (NYSED)
NYSED posts a statewide Property Tax Report Card workbook each year (https://www.p12.nysed.gov/mgtserv/propertytax/) recording, for every district, the proposed budget, the proposed and prior-year tax levy, the district’s tax levy limit, projected enrollment, and reserve balances — the numbers put before voters each May. These are proposed/budgeted figures (forward- looking), in contrast to the Comptroller’s actual spending. Provenance and the year-by-year download list: data/raw/nysed_property_tax_report_card/SOURCE.md.

title: Sources & references
text:
School district property taxes (NYS Comptroller)
To show how much property tax each school district raises and how heavily it taxes property, the project uses the Comptroller’s “Table 3” school-district tax workbooks (the same OSC local-government data program as the finance files, at https://www.osc.ny.gov/local-government/data/real-property-tax-levies-taxable-full-value-and-full-value-tax-rates). For every district and each year 2015 through 2025, they give the district’s tax levy (total property tax the district raises), its taxable full value (the market-value tax base, after equalizing assessed values across towns), and the full-value tax rate — the levy per $1,000 of full value, the standard way to compare tax effort across places with different assessment practices.
A distinctive feature: each district’s figures are broken out by the city or town the district covers — many districts straddle several towns — so the data has both a detail row per district-and-town and a district total. The totals are summed from the parts (and match the Comptroller’s own published totals, where it publishes them, to the dollar). The fiscal year ends June 30 and matches the school-year-ending label, the same as the finance data (verified against the Property Tax Report Card). Districts are joined to the project’s key via a generated crosswalk; 678 of 680 link, the two exceptions being New York City (which the state splits into many borough districts) and one non-operating district. Full provenance, the format-change history, and caveats (including a 2019-file quirk) are in data/raw/osc_school_district_tax_levies/SOURCE.md.

title: Sources & references
text:
Cambridge CSD annual financial reports
Cambridge’s audited annual financial statements, for fiscal years ended June 30, 2020 through June 30, 2025, were retrieved from the Federal Audit Clearinghouse (https://app.fac.gov/). The clearinghouse was operated by the U.S. Census Bureau through about 2022 and by the U.S. General Services Administration from 2023 onward — which is why the earlier files (FY2020–FY2022) carry a CENSUS tag and bear the title “Financial statements and independent auditor’s report,” while the later files (FY2023–FY2025) carry a GSAFAC tag and the title “Financial report.” They are the same series of district audits.

FY2020 (Cambridge Central School District, 2020), FY2021 (Cambridge Central School District, 2021), FY2022 (Cambridge Central School District, 2022)
FY2023 (Cambridge Central School District, 2023), FY2024 (Cambridge Central School District, 2024), FY2025 (Cambridge Central School District, 2025)

title: Sources & references
text:
Candidate sources under consideration
These are not yet used on the website but are likely additions, both from New York’s open-data portal (https://data.ny.gov):

State school aid (dataset 9pb8-dg53) — state aid to each school district by year, back to the mid-1990s. Would let the spending chapter separate state-funded from locally-funded dollars.
Real property tax rates & levy data by municipality (dataset iq85-sdzs) — tax levies and rates by municipality, useful for putting each district’s school tax levy in the context of total local property taxes.

title: Sources & references
text:
Reader comments
Each page of this website has a comment area at the bottom, powered by Giscus. To leave a comment or a reaction, sign in with a free GitHub account; comments are stored as discussions in this website’s GitHub repository.

title: Sources & references
